title: Autocomplete on an enormous scale
content: Your phone keyboard suggests the next word as you type. A large language model - an LLM - uses a similar basic idea at far larger scale: given some text, predict what comes next. Much of what an LLM does - answering questions, writing code, translating - emerges from repeating that prediction step over and over.
next: But what exactly is a 'piece' of text?

title: Tokens: the LEGO bricks of language
content: LLMs do not work directly on letters or full words. Text is split into tokens - common chunks such as 'the', 'ing', or 'walk'. A token is often smaller than a full word, though the exact size varies. Each token is converted into numbers, because the model ultimately works through mathematical operations on numeric representations.
next: So how does one token become a whole answer?

title: One token at a time
content: The model reads your prompt and predicts a single next token. That token is stuck onto the text, and the whole thing is fed back in to predict the next one. Then again, and again, dozens of times per second. Like building a sentence one fridge magnet at a time - except the choice of each magnet takes into account everything placed before it.
next: How does it know which token is likely? That took some serious schooling.

title: Training: billions of flashcards
content: During training, the model processes very large amounts of text - books, articles, websites, and code - and repeats the same task constantly: guess the next token, compare with the real next token, and adjust when wrong. Repeating this at huge scale is how the model learns statistical patterns in language.
next: Where do all those adjustments actually get stored?

title: Parameters: billions of tiny knobs
content: A model's learned behavior is stored in its parameters - numbers adjusted during training, like billions of tiny knobs on a huge control board. Modern LLMs can have billions or even hundreds of billions of parameters. When people say a model is 'bigger', they often mean it has more parameters and therefore more capacity to capture subtle patterns.
next: Training builds the model. Using it is a different phase entirely.

title: Training vs inference
content: Training happens once, costs enormous amounts of money and computing power, and takes months. Inference is what happens every time you chat: the finished model runs your prompt through its parameters to produce tokens. Think cooking school versus cooking dinner - years of expensive schooling, then fast daily use of what was learned.
next: When you chat, how much can the model actually keep in mind?

title: The context window: a desk of fixed size
content: The context window is how much text the model can consider at once - prompt plus answer, measured in tokens. Picture a desk of fixed size: you can lay out this many pages, no more. If the conversation outgrows the desk, the oldest pages fall off. Bigger context windows are one of the main ways models have improved.
next: There is also a dial that controls how adventurous the answers get.

title: Temperature: the adventurousness dial
content: For each token, the model has many candidates with different probabilities. Temperature affects how strongly it prefers the most likely option. Low temperature usually gives more predictable answers. High temperature allows less likely tokens more often, which can make output more creative but also less stable. It is a useful control for variation.
next: This probability game explains AI's most famous flaw.

title: Hallucination: confident guessing
content: An LLM predicts plausible text, not verified truth. When it does not know something, it does not go silent - it produces the most plausible-sounding continuation, complete with confident tone and made-up details. This is called hallucination. It is not lying; it is a probability machine doing exactly what it was built to do, on a question it cannot actually answer.
next: Newer models fight this with a surprisingly human strategy.

title: Reasoning models: showing their work
content: Reasoning-oriented models often spend more computation on intermediate steps before producing a final answer. This can look like working through a problem in stages instead of answering immediately. The extra work costs time and tokens, but it can improve performance on harder problems in math, logic, and code.
next: And what if you need a model tuned for your specific field?

title: Fine-tuning: from generalist to specialist
content: Fine-tuning takes a fully trained model and gives it extra training on a focused dataset - medical texts, legal contracts, a company's support tickets. Like a doctor completing a specialization after medical school. The model keeps its general abilities but gets sharper in the chosen domain.
next: So if this is the engine - what is ChatGPT, then?

title: The model is not the product
content: Everything in this module - tokens, parameters, context, temperature - describes the raw model. But nobody chats with a raw model. Claude and ChatGPT are applications wrapped around LLMs, adding memory, history, tools, and a friendly interface. That wrapper is the next piece of the puzzle.
next: Next: why ChatGPT is not GPT, and why the difference matters.
